chunk_summary: Guidance on selecting post-discharge feeding modalities for preterm/LBW/ELBW infants, focusing on safety, endurance, and resource constraints, with an emphasis on breastfeeding and alternative feeding methods.
learning_objectives: Understand the physiological factors influencing post-discharge feeding modality selection for preterm/LBW/ELBW infants.
Learn safe practices for preparing and delivering breast milk and formula in resource-constrained settings.
Identify signs of feeding inefficiency or aspiration and determine when tube feeding is necessary.
Explore infection risk mitigation strategies for oral feeding methods in low-resource environments.
key_facts: Mother’s milk is preferred for VLBW/ELBW infants and should be fortified when indicated.
Powdered formula is not sterile; improper preparation or over-concentration can be unsafe.
Cup or spoon/paladai feeding may be safer than bottle feeding in areas with sanitation constraints.
Tube feeding is recommended for recurrent aspiration, fatigue during feeding, or inability to meet daily intake needs.
Operational safety for tube feeding includes gravity feeds, continuous observation, and stopping feeds for signs of distress (e.g., cyanosis, vomiting).
Exclusive breastfeeding with prolonged feeds (e.g., 40–45 minutes) may indicate inefficiency and require intervention.
reference_quote: Powdered formula is not sterile and over-concentration is unsafe. (Source: WHO, Feeding of low birth weight babies)
document_key_topics: Post-discharge feeding modalities for preterm/LBW/ELBW infants
Safety and efficiency in breastfeeding and formula feeding
Tube feeding protocols and operational safety
Resource-constrained feeding methods and infection risk
